docs.title: AP World History: Modern
docs.text:
Welcome to the AP World History: Modern intelligent textbook project.

This site contains course notes, a detailed course description, and supporting learning materials for a world history curriculum aligned with the College Board AP World History: Modern framework (c. 1200 CE to the present).

  • Course Description — full scope, topics, and Bloom's Taxonomy learning outcomes
  • World History Notes — reference notes on frameworks and standards
  • Learning Graph — concept maps and assessment reports

docs.title: AP World History: Modern
docs.text:
Title: World History: 1200CE to Present

Target Audience: High school students (grades 10–12) seeking college-equivalent coursework; the course is aligned with introductory college-level modern world history.

Prerequisites: None. A general comfort with reading primary and secondary historical sources and writing analytical essays is helpful but not required.

docs.title: Course Overview
docs.text:
World History: Modern is an introductory college-level survey of world history from approximately 1200 CE to the present. Rather than memorizing isolated facts, students cultivate the habits of mind of professional historians: analyzing primary and secondary sources, evaluating competing interpretations, contextualizing events within broader patterns of change, and constructing evidence-based arguments about the past.

The course is organized into nine thematic units that move chronologically from the interconnected civilizations of the medieval world through the emergence of global trade networks, the rise and fall of land-based and maritime empires, the revolutionary upheavals of the 18th and 19th centuries, the catastrophic conflicts of the 20th century, and the accelerating forces of globalization in the contemporary era. Six recurring themes — humans and the environment, cultural developments and interactions, governance, economic systems, social interactions and organization, and technology and innovation — thread through every unit, allowing students to trace long-run patterns across time and geography.

College Board recognizes AP World History: Modern as equivalent to an introductory college course, and qualifying exam scores (typically 3–5) are accepted for college credit or advanced placement at thousands of universities. The course therefore serves a dual purpose: building genuine historical literacy and critical thinking while creating a measurable credential that strengthens college applications and can reduce time and cost in higher education.

There are several cross-cutting themes in this course:

  1. Development of critical thinking skills
  2. Cultivation of systems thinking to understand world events holistically
  3. Healthy skepticism about historical claims, including the ability to detect bias and misinformation across sources

docs.title: Unit 1: The Global Tapestry (c. 1200–c. 1450) — 8–10% of exam
docs.text:
• State formation and political structures in East Asia (Song, Yuan, Ming dynasties)
  • Dar al-Islam: Abbasid Caliphate, regional sultanates, and Islamic governance
  • South and Southeast Asian kingdoms (Delhi Sultanate, Vijayanagara, Majapahit)
  • African states (Great Zimbabwe, Mali Empire, Swahili city-states)
  • Americas civilizations (Aztec/Mexica, Inca, Maya)
  • European feudalism, the Catholic Church, and emerging monarchies
  • Global religions: Islam, Christianity, Buddhism, Hinduism, and their spread

docs.title: Unit 2: Networks of Exchange (c. 1200–c. 1450) — 8–10% of exam
docs.text:
• The Silk Roads: overland trade routes and cultural diffusion
  • The Mongol Empire and Pax Mongolica
  • Indian Ocean trade network: merchants, monsoons, and port cities
  • Trans-Saharan trade routes and the gold-salt economy
  • The Black Death: origins, spread, and demographic consequences
  • Diasporic merchant communities and cross-cultural exchange

docs.title: Unit 3: Land-Based Empires (c. 1450–c. 1750) — 12–15% of exam
docs.text:
• Ottoman Empire: administrative structures, devshirme system, expansion
  • Safavid Empire: Shia Islam as state religion, relations with Ottomans
  • Mughal Empire: religious policy, administration, Akbar's syncretism
  • Manchu (Qing) Empire: conquest, governance of Han Chinese subjects
  • Methods of empire-building: military technology, bureaucracy, tax systems
  • Religion and legitimacy: how rulers used faith to consolidate power

docs.title: Unit 4: Transoceanic Interconnections (c. 1450–c. 1750) — 12–15% of exam
docs.text:
• European maritime exploration: Portuguese, Spanish, Dutch, English, French
  • The Columbian Exchange: crops, animals, diseases, and demographic collapse
  • Atlantic slave trade: origins, scale, Middle Passage, African participation
  • Colonial economies: encomienda, mita, plantation systems
  • Joint-stock companies (VOC, British East India Company) and mercantilism
  • Cultural and biological mixing: mestizaje, creole societies, syncretism
  • Shifting social hierarchies in colonial contexts

docs.title: Unit 5: Revolutions (c. 1750–c. 1900) — 12–15% of exam
docs.text:
• Enlightenment ideas: natural rights, social contract, reason, secularism
  • Atlantic Revolutions: American, French, Haitian, Latin American independence movements
  • Nationalism and the nation-state
  • The Industrial Revolution: origins in Britain, spread to Europe and North America
  • Technological change: steam power, railroads, textile machinery, steel
  • New economic ideologies: capitalism, socialism, communism (Marx and Engels)
  • Labor movements and changing working conditions

docs.title: Unit 6: Consequences of Industrialization (c. 1750–c. 1900) — 12–15% of exam
docs.text:
• New imperialism: motives, methods, and ideological justifications
  • Colonization of Africa (the Scramble for Africa, Berlin Conference)
  • British Raj in India and its economic impact
  • Imperialism in East and Southeast Asia: Opium Wars, Meiji Japan's response
  • Indigenous resistance movements: Sepoy Rebellion, Zulu resistance, Boxer Uprising
  • Economic dependency and the world-systems framework
  • Environmental consequences of industrial extraction

docs.title: Unit 7: Global Conflict (c. 1900–present) — 8–10% of exam
docs.text:
• Causes of World War I: nationalism, imperialism, militarism, alliances
  • New warfare technologies: machine guns, poison gas, trench warfare, tanks
  • Russian Revolution and the rise of the Soviet Union
  • The interwar period: Great Depression, rise of fascism, Japanese expansionism
  • World War II: causes, theaters of conflict, the Holocaust, atomic bombs
  • Mass atrocities and genocide as a 20th-century phenomenon
  • The United Nations and post-war international order

docs.title: Unit 8: Cold War and Decolonization (c. 1900–present) — 8–10% of exam
docs.text:
• Cold War origins: Truman Doctrine, Marshall Plan, NATO vs. Warsaw Pact
  • Proxy wars: Korea, Vietnam, Angola, Afghanistan
  • Nuclear arms race and deterrence doctrine
  • Decolonization in Asia: Indian independence, partition, Vietnamese independence
  • Decolonization in Africa: pan-Africanism, independence movements, challenges
  • Non-Aligned Movement and the "Third World"
  • Collapse of the Soviet Union and the end of the Cold War

docs.title: Unit 9: Globalization (c. 1900–present) — 8–10% of exam
docs.text:
• Technological revolutions: computers, the internet, mobile communication
  • Economic globalization: multinational corporations, free trade agreements, supply chains
  • Global financial institutions: IMF, World Bank, WTO
  • Migration and diaspora communities
  • Climate change and environmental degradation as global challenges
  • Cultural globalization: spread of popular culture, language dominance, resistance
  • Pandemic disease in the 20th–21st centuries
  • Rise of China and the shifting global order

docs.title: Topics Not Covered
docs.text:
• Ancient history (pre-1200 CE): early civilizations, classical antiquity, the ancient Mediterranean — these belong to AP World History: Ancient or comparable courses
  • Deep national history of individual countries beyond their role in global networks (e.g., detailed US domestic political history, detailed Chinese dynastic internal politics beyond their global relevance)
  • AP US History or AP European History content, which treat their respective regions with much greater granularity
  • Pre-1200 CE religious developments, though post-1200 spread and transformation of religions is covered
  • Detailed military tactics, campaigns, or battles (the focus is on causes and consequences, not operational history)
  • Economic theory beyond historical context (no formal macroeconomics or microeconomics)
  • Geography as a standalone discipline (though geographic literacy is assumed)

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text: After completing this course, students will be able to:

docs.title: Remember
docs.text:
Retrieving, recognizing, and recalling relevant knowledge from long-term memory.

  • Recall the nine AP World History: Modern units and their approximate date ranges
  • Identify the six core course themes: humans and the environment, cultural developments and interactions, governance, economic systems, social interactions and organization, and technology and innovation
  • Name the major land-based empires of 1450–1750 (Ottoman, Safavid, Mughal, Qing) and their geographic locations
  • Recall key trade routes of the pre-modern world: Silk Roads, Indian Ocean network, trans-Saharan routes
  • List the major causes and phases of World War I and World War II
  • Identify significant historical actors (rulers, thinkers, revolutionaries) and their associated movements or time periods
  • Define key vocabulary: mercantilism, encomienda, devshirme, Columbian Exchange, Pax Mongolica, decolonization, proxy war, globalization
  • Recall the structure of the AP exam (MCQ, SAQ, DBQ, Long Essay) and the historical thinking skills assessed

docs.title: Understand
docs.text:
Constructing meaning from instructional messages, including oral, written, and graphic communication.

  • Explain how the Columbian Exchange reshaped demographics, agriculture, and economies across multiple continents
  • Describe how Enlightenment ideas about natural rights and popular sovereignty fueled Atlantic revolutions
  • Summarize how industrialization in Britain spread to other regions and transformed labor, urbanship, and global trade
  • Explain the relationship between European imperialism and economic dependency in Africa and Asia
  • Describe how the Cold War rivalry between the US and USSR shaped decolonization movements and proxy conflicts
  • Interpret historical maps, charts, and images as evidence of broader historical processes
  • Explain why the Mongol Empire simultaneously facilitated trade and caused catastrophic violence and disease spread
  • Summarize how different empires used religion, bureaucracy, and military force to maintain control over diverse populations

docs.title: Apply
docs.text:
Carrying out or using a procedure in a given situation.

  • Apply the six historical thinking skills (developments and processes, sourcing and situation, claims and evidence, contextualization, making connections, argumentation) when analyzing primary sources
  • Use the HAPP-Y sourcing framework (Historical context, Audience, Purpose, Point of view, and corroboration) to evaluate primary source documents
  • Apply the continuity and change over time (CCOT) reasoning process to trace how a theme evolved across multiple periods
  • Write a thesis statement that makes a historically defensible claim and establishes a line of reasoning
  • Apply comparative analysis to explain similarities and differences between two or more civilizations, empires, or revolutions
  • Categorize historical evidence by type (primary/secondary, quantitative/qualitative) and assess its reliability for a specific argument
  • Construct a short-answer response using specific historical evidence to support a claim

docs.title: Analyze
docs.text:
Breaking material into constituent parts and determining how the parts relate to one another and to an overall structure or purpose.

  • Analyze how geography shaped the development and reach of pre-modern trade networks
  • Analyze the causes of the Industrial Revolution by breaking them into technological, economic, social, and political factors
  • Deconstruct a Document-Based Question (DBQ) prompt by identifying what evidence is needed, what perspective is missing, and how documents corroborate or contradict one another
  • Analyze how nationalist movements in colonized regions drew on and diverged from Enlightenment political theory
  • Examine how social hierarchies (race, gender, class, caste) were constructed, maintained, and contested across different historical contexts
  • Break down the argument structure of a historian's interpretation to identify the thesis, evidence used, and assumptions made
  • Analyze how technological innovations (gunpowder, printing press, steam engine, internet) changed political and economic power structures

docs.title: Evaluate
docs.text:
Making judgments based on criteria and standards through checking and critiquing.

  • Evaluate the reliability and usefulness of a primary source based on its origin, purpose, and historical context
  • Judge the relative significance of multiple causes of a major event (e.g., World War I, the Industrial Revolution)
  • Assess the extent to which a historical claim is supported by available evidence
  • Evaluate competing historical interpretations about contested events (e.g., the role of the slave trade in African development, the legacy of colonialism)
  • Critique the perspective and limitations of official or government-produced historical documents
  • Evaluate the degree to which an empire's administrative policies contributed to its stability or eventual decline
  • Assess how well a student or model essay meets the AP rubric criteria for thesis, contextualization, evidence, and complexity

docs.title: Create
docs.text:
Putting elements together to form a coherent or functional whole; reorganizing elements into a new pattern or structure.

  • Write a fully developed Document-Based Question essay that integrates at least six documents, adds outside evidence, contextualizes the argument, and demonstrates complexity
  • Compose a Long Essay that constructs an original argument, supports it with specific evidence, and explains causation or comparison across time periods
  • Design a comparative timeline that places developments from multiple regions side by side to reveal patterns of convergence and divergence
  • Develop an original historical argument about a topic not directly covered in class, using the analytical skills practiced throughout the course
  • Create an annotated bibliography of primary and secondary sources on a chosen historical controversy
  • Produce a capstone research project or presentation that synthesizes evidence from multiple units to answer a cross-cutting historical question (e.g., "How did disease shape the outcome of colonial encounters across different regions?" or "What role did trade networks play in spreading both ideas and pathogens from 1200 to the present?")
  • Design a thematic essay that traces one of the six course themes across all nine units, demonstrating change over time and continuity

docs.title: Assessment and Exam Format
docs.text:
The AP exam is administered digitally via the Bluebook app and consists of:

Section Format Time Weight Section I-A 55 Multiple Choice Questions 55 min 40% Section I-B 3 Short Answer Questions 40 min 20% Section II 1 Document-Based Question 60 min 25% Section II 1 Long Essay (choice of 3) 40 min 15%

docs.title: Why This Course Matters
docs.text: AP World History: Modern equips students with skills that transfer far beyond the history classroom. The ability to evaluate sources critically, identify the difference between evidence and assertion, construct a coherent argument, and understand how present conditions were shaped by historical forces are foundational competencies for college coursework in virtually every discipline — from political science and economics to literature and the natural sciences. In an era when information is abundant but poorly contextualized, the analytical habits built in this course are among the most durable investments a high school student can make.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
• AP Central: https://apcentral.collegeboard.org/courses/ap-world-history
  • AP Students: https://apstudents.collegeboard.org/courses/ap-world-history-modern
  • Course and Exam Description (CED): https://apcentral.collegeboard.org/media/pdf/ap-world-history-modern-course-and-exam-description.pdf

docs.title: World History Course Notes
docs.text: For a world history course aimed at building college credibility while strengthening critical thinking, you'll want to draw from a layered set of standards: high-credibility frameworks that colleges explicitly recognize, plus pedagogy-focused frameworks that emphasize inquiry. Here are the major organizations to anchor your work.

docs.title: College Board — AP World History: Modern
docs.text:
This is the framework with the most direct college admissions credibility, since AP courses are designed as college-equivalent and many universities grant credit for qualifying scores. The course covers world history from c. 1200 CE to the present and is organized around themes including humans and the environment, cultural developments and interactions, governance, economic systems, social interactions, and technology and innovation. The framework has two essential components — historical thinking skills/reasoning processes, and course content organized into commonly taught units. The full Course and Exam Description (CED) is the authoritative concept list.

AP Central: https://apcentral.collegeboard.org/courses/ap-world-history

docs.title: 1. Overall Score: 94 / 100
docs.text: Quality Rating: Excellent — Ready for learning graph generation

docs.title: 2. Detailed Scoring Breakdown
docs.text: Element Points Possible Points Earned Notes Title 5 5 Clear, descriptive, matches official AP designation Target Audience 5 5 Specific: high school grades 10–12, college-equivalent Prerequisites 5 5 Explicitly stated as "None" with helpful context Main Topics Covered 10 10 9 fully elaborated units with sub-topics; exceeds the 5–10 topic baseline Topics Excluded 5 5 7 clearly defined exclusions with rationale Learning Outcomes Header 5 5 Present and correctly formatted Remember Level 10 10 8 specific, measurable outcomes with named vocabulary Understand Level 10 10 8 outcomes covering key causal and conceptual explanations Apply Level 10 10 7 outcomes tied to AP-specific skill procedures (sourcing, CCOT, thesis writing) Analyze Level 10 9 7 strong outcomes; could add one more on social history analysis Evaluate Level 10 10 7 outcomes including source critique and rubric assessment Create Level 10 10 7 outcomes including capstone research project and thematic essay Descriptive Context 5 5 Strong "Why This Course Matters" section with transferable-skills framing TOTAL 100 94

docs.title: Minor Gaps (items scoring below full points)
docs.text: Analyze Level (9/10) - The seven Analyze outcomes are strong, but there is an opportunity to add one outcome focused on social history analysis — specifically how race, gender, and class hierarchies intersect across different regional contexts (e.g., analyzing how colonial caste systems differed across Spanish America, British India, and French West Africa). This would broaden the analytical frame beyond political/economic causation.

docs.title: No Major Gaps Found
docs.text: All other elements are complete and high-quality. The course description exceeds minimum requirements in most categories.

docs.title: Priority 1 (Minor — Analyze level)
docs.text:
Add one Analyze outcome such as:

"Analyze how intersecting social categories (race, gender, religion, class) structured opportunity and resistance differently across colonial contexts in Asia, Africa, and the Americas."

docs.title: Priority 2 (Optional enrichment)
docs.text: The Topics Not Covered section is excellent. Consider adding one line noting that post-2001 events (the War on Terror, the 2008 financial crisis, the COVID-19 pandemic) are not formally tested on the AP exam even though Unit 9 nominally extends to "the present." This sets accurate expectations for students.

docs.title: Priority 3 (Optional enrichment)
docs.text: The Assessment section could note that the Long Essay exam choices span three different time windows (pre-1450 / 1450–1750 / post-1750) so students can expect to need evidence from at least two units per essay.

docs.title: Estimated Concept Count
docs.text:
Based on the current course description, the following concept counts are projected:

Source Estimated Concepts Unit sub-topics (9 units × ~10 sub-topics each) ~90 Key vocabulary and named events ~40 Historical figures and actors ~25 Bloom's Taxonomy learning outcomes (distinct skills/processes) ~30 Cross-cutting themes and analytical frameworks ~20 Total estimated ~205

The course description comfortably supports 200+ concepts. The nine richly elaborated units, seven explicit exclusions (which help focus the concept space), and six Bloom's levels with specific action verbs all contribute to a well-defined concept space.

docs.title: Diversity of Concept Types
docs.text:
• Factual concepts: empires, events, treaties, figures, dates
  • Conceptual abstractions: mercantilism, nationalism, decolonization, globalization
  • Process concepts: Columbian Exchange, industrial diffusion, Cold War proxy dynamics
  • Skill concepts: sourcing, CCOT, DBQ construction, thesis development
  • Evaluative concepts: historical significance, source reliability, argument quality

This diversity is ideal for a learning graph with multiple node types and edge relationships.

docs.title: 6. Next Steps
docs.text:
Score ≥ 85 — Ready to proceed with learning graph generation.

Recommended next actions: 1. ✅ Add course-description.md to mkdocs.yml navigation 2. ✅ Add this assessment file to mkdocs.yml under a Learning Graph section 3. Run the learning-graph-generator skill to generate 200+ concepts and their relationships 4. Consider running the glossary-generator skill to build a vocabulary reference from the ~40 key terms identified in the Remember outcomes

docs.title: Seed Prompt
docs.text: !!! prompt I would like to create a course on world history for high-school students. What organizations provide lists of concepts that should be covered in a world history course. My goal is to create a course that has high credibility for my students as they apply for colleges. I also want to promote critical thinking.

docs.title: Response
docs.text: For a world history course aimed at building college credibility while strengthening critical thinking, you'll want to draw from a layered set of standards: high-credibility frameworks that colleges explicitly recognize, plus pedagogy-focused frameworks that emphasize inquiry. Here are the major organizations to anchor your work.

docs.title: Tier 1 — Frameworks colleges recognize directly
docs.text: College Board — AP World History: Modern. This is the framework with the most direct college admissions credibility, since AP courses are designed as college-equivalent and many universities grant credit for qualifying scores. The course covers world history from c. 1200 CE to the present and is organized around themes including humans and the environment, cultural developments and interactions, governance, economic systems, social interactions, and technology and innovation. The framework has two essential components — historical thinking skills/reasoning processes, and course content organized into commonly taught units. The full Course and Exam Description (CED) is the authoritative concept list. AP Central: https://apcentral.collegeboard.org/courses/ap-world-history College BoardCollege Board

docs.title: International Baccalaureate (IB) — History (Diploma Programme).
docs.text: The other internationally recognized college-credibility framework. The IB History guide specifies prescribed subjects, world history topics, and HL regional options, and is built around historical investigation and source analysis — a strong match for critical thinking. IB: https://www.ibo.org/programmes/diploma-programme/curriculum/individuals-and-societies/history/

docs.title: National Council for the Social Studies (NCSS) — C3 Framework
docs.text: The College, Career, and Civic Life Framework was developed to help states upgrade their social studies standards and to help districts and curriculum writers strengthen their programs, with explicit goals of building critical thinking, problem solving, and participatory skills for engaged citizenship. It uses an inquiry arc of four dimensions, organized around compelling and supporting questions to guide investigation and application. This is the strongest framework for the critical-thinking goal. NCSS also publishes the National Curriculum Standards for Social Studies (10 themes). https://www.socialstudies.org/standards/c3 Social StudiesSocial Studies

docs.title: National Center for History in the Schools (NCHS), UCLA
docs.text: National Standards for History. These standards call for a minimum of three years of world history instruction between grades 5 and 12 and advocate courses that are genuinely global in scope. Older (1996) but still influential as a content reference. https://phi.history.ucla.edu/nchs/ Ucla

docs.title: American Historical Association (AHA).
docs.text: The largest U.S. professional history organization. The AHA History Tuning Project defines what students should know and be able to do as historians, and the AHA collaborated on the C3 Framework. Useful for grounding your concept list in what professional historians regard as core. https://www.historians.org/

docs.title: World History Association (WHA).
docs.text: The dedicated professional society for world historians. Useful for periodization debates and globally balanced framing (avoiding Eurocentrism), which colleges increasingly look for. https://www.thewha.org/

docs.title: Tier 3 — Pedagogy-focused, free, standards-aligned
docs.text:
OER Project (World History Project / Big History Project). Founded by historian David Christian and funded by Bill Gates. The curricula are aligned to state standards and develop key historical thinking skills, with student critical thinking and questioning placed front and center. Free, modular, and explicitly designed to feed into AP. Particularly strong for the critical-thinking dimension and for providing a concept graph–like backbone (compelling questions, threshold concepts) that should map well to the learning-graph approach you use in your textbooks. https://www.oerproject.com/World-History OER Project

Stanford History Education Group (SHEG) — Reading Like a Historian. Not a content standards body, but the canonical source for sourcing, contextualization, corroboration, and close reading exercises that operationalize critical thinking. https://sheg.stanford.edu/ State frameworks worth comparing State frameworks vary in rigor but several are widely respected and well-documented: Virginia Standards of Learning (used by Fairfax County), California History–Social Science Framework (2016), Maryland Modern World History Framework, and the New York State K–12 Social Studies Framework. Minnesota also publishes its own academic standards in social studies that you'll likely need to reference for local credibility.

docs.title: A practical recommendation
docs.text:
For maximum college credibility plus critical thinking, I'd suggest using the AP World History: Modern CED as your primary content spine, then overlaying the C3 Framework's inquiry arc as your pedagogical structure, and pulling OER Project / SHEG materials for activities and primary-source work. That combination gives you (a) a defensible concept list colleges recognize, (b) an inquiry-based skeleton that demands critical thinking rather than memorization, and (c) ready-to-adapt open materials.

Given your work on concept dependencies and learning graphs, the AP CED's Key Concepts and the C3 Indicators are both already structured hierarchically and would translate well into a Bloom's-tagged learning graph. Want me to sketch what that graph structure might look like for one period (e.g., 1200–1450 CE)?
